lines.text: APPENDIX
Key Nobel Prizes mentioned:
1933 — Thomas Hunt Morgan, "for his discoveries concerning the role played by the chromosome in heredity.
" Drosophila.
1995 — Edward Lewis, Christiane Nüsslein-Volhard, Eric Wieschaus, "for their discoveries concerning the genetic control of early embryonic development.
" Drosophila (Heidelberg screen).
2002 — Sydney Brenner, John Sulston, Robert Horvitz, "for their discoveries concerning genetic regulation of organ development and programmed cell death.
" C. elegans.
2006 — Andrew Fire, Craig Mello, "for their discovery of RNA interference — gene silencing by double-stranded RNA.
" C. elegans.
2017 — Jeffrey Hall, Michael Rosbash, Michael Young, "for their discoveries of molecular mechanisms controlling the circadian rhythm.
" Drosophila.
Key genes by organism:
C. elegans: unc-86 (neuronal fate), lin-4/lin-14 (first microRNA), ced-3/ced-4/ced-9 (apoptosis), daf-2/age-1/daf-16 (insulin signaling and aging), unc-13 (synaptic transmission).

Drosophila: white (X-linkage and ABC transporters), Antennapedia/Bithorax (homeotic transformation), bicoid/nanos (maternal axis), Krüppel/hunchback (gap genes), even-skipped/fushi tarazu (pair-rule), engrailed/wingless/hedgehog (segment polarity), period/timeless/doubletime (circadian clock), dunce (learning), fruitless (courtship).
Zebrafish: one-eyed pinhead (Nodal signaling), cyclops (hedgehog), no tail (T-box), silberblick (Wnt), gata4/nkx2.5 (heart development), casper (transparent adult).
Suggested reading: Brenner's 1974 paper "The Genetics of Caenorhabditis elegans" (Genetics 77:71).
Sulston et al. 1983 "The embryonic cell lineage of the nematode Caenorhabditis elegans" (Dev Biol 100:64).
Nüsslein-Volhard and Wieschaus 1980 "Mutations affecting segment number and polarity in Drosophila" (Nature 287:795).
Fire et al. 1998 "Potent and specific genetic interference by double-stranded RNA in Caenorhabditis elegans" (Nature 391:806).
Kenyon et al. 1993 "A C. elegans mutant that lives twice as long as wild type" (Nature 366:461).
The Boston-Tübingen 1996 special issue of Development (volume 123).
And, for an accessible history, In the Beginning Was the Worm by Andrew Brown.

Cross-references in this book series: the mouse models book extends the small-animal pipeline into mammals; the RNAi/siRNA book covers Fire and Mello's discovery in mechanistic detail; the CRISPR book covers the technology that revolutionized fish reverse genetics; the Cre-Lox book covers tissue-specific genetic manipulation, parallel to the UAS-Gal4 system in flies; the single-cell book covers the transcriptomic technologies being applied to fish embryos to build cell-by-cell vertebrate
atlases.
